board-07_0h13m35s.png:
\section*{Recall: Tree = connected + acyclic (original definition)}
Theorem: Let \( G \) be a finite graph with \( n \) vertices and \( m \) edges. The following are equivalent:
(1) \( G \) is connected and acyclic.
(2) \( G \) is connected and \( m = n - 1 \).
(3) \( G \) is acyclic and \( m = n - 1 \).

Strategy: Prove (1) \(\Rightarrow\) (2) \(\Rightarrow\) (3) \(\Rightarrow\) (1)

\begin{align*}
&\text{(1)} \\
&\text{(2)}
\end{align*}

Proof:
(1) \(\Rightarrow\) (2):
Assume \( G \) is connected and acyclic (tree). Last time, longest path trick. \( G \) must have a leaf. Remove a leaf & do induction to show that \( G \) has exactly \( m = n - 1 \) edges.

(2) \(\Rightarrow\) (3):
Assume \( G \) is connected and \( m = n - 1 \). Goal: Show \( G \) is acyclic. Proof: Assume, to the contrary, \( G \) has a cycle. Remove an edge from that cycle to obtain a smaller connected graph. If this one still has a cycle, continue removing (one edge at a time).

\section*{Proof:}
(1) \(\Rightarrow\) (2):
Assume \( G \) is connected and acyclic (tree). Last time, longest path trick. \( G \) must have a leaf. Remove a leaf & do induction to show that \( G \) has exactly \( m = n - 1 \) edges.

(2) \(\Rightarrow\) (3):
Assume \( G \) is connected and \( m = n - 1 \). Goal: Show \( G \) is acyclic. Proof: Assume, to the contrary, \( G \) has a cycle. Remove an edge from that cycle to obtain a smaller connected graph. If this one still has a cycle, continue removing (one edge at a time).

(3) \( G \) is acyclic and \( m = n - 1 \).

&\text{This proves (2).}
\end{align*}

(2) \(\Rightarrow\) (3): Assume \( G \) is connected and \( m = n - 1 \).

\section*{Recall: Tree = connected + acyclic (original definition)}
Theorem: Let \( G \) be a finite graph with \( n \) vertices and \( m \) edges. The following are equivalent:
(1) \( G \) is connected and acyclic.
(2) \( G \) is connected and \( m = n - 1 \).
(3) \( G \) is acyclic and \( m = n - 1 \).

Strategy: Prove (1) \(\Rightarrow\) (2) \(\Rightarrow\) (3) \(\Rightarrow\) (1)

\begin{align*}
&\text{(1)} \implies \text{(2)} \\
&\text{Assume } G \text{ is connected and acyclic (tree).} \\
&\text{Last time, longest path trick: \( G \) must have a leaf.} \\
&\text{Remove a leaf & do induction to show that \( G \) has exactly } m = n - 1 \text{ edges. This proves (2).}
\end{align*}

(2) \(\Rightarrow\) (3): Assume \( G \) is connected and \( m = n - 1 \).

Goal: Show \( G \) is acyclic.

Proof: Assume, to the contrary, \( G \) has a cycle. Remove an edge from that cycle to obtain a smaller connected graph. If this one still has a cycle, continue removing (one edge at a time).

\section*{Plot: (1) \(\Rightarrow\) (2).}
Continue breaking cycles (one at a time) until we get a tree.

board-27_0h45m40s.png:
\section*{Definition}
Let \( G \) be a connected graph. A spanning tree of \( G \) is a tree \( T \subseteq G \) (\( T \) is a subgraph) such that \( V(T) = V(G) \).

\[ 
T \quad \text{(tree)} \quad G
\]

Theorem 1: Let \( G \) be a connected graph with \( n \geqslant 2 \) vertices. Then there exists a vertex \( v \) in \( G \) such that \( G - v \) is still connected.

Proof:
Let \( T \) be a spanning tree of \( G \). Let \( v \) be a leaf of \( T \), that is, \( \deg_T(v) = 1 \). Then \( T - v \) is connected (because it is a tree).

\[ 
V(T-v) = V(G-v) \quad \text{and} \quad E(T-v) \subseteq E(G-v)
\]

Thus, \( G - v \) is also connected.

\section*{Theorem 2:}
Every connected graph \( G \) has a spanning tree.

Proof:
Remove an edge from a cycle (repeatedly) until no more cycles exist. The final subgraph is a spanning tree.

\section*{Today:}
Characterization of Trees and Spanning Trees

Let \( G = (V(G), E(G)) \) be a graph.
A subset \( D \subseteq V(G) \) is called a dominating set if for every vertex \( v \in G \),
